text: Adsorption and separation of gases on porous materials is an economically important process and a fundamental materials design challenge, where the adsorbent properties can be engineered to selectively adsorb gases. The burgeoning field of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offers a unique opportunity to design new materials with functional properties. The huge number of possible MOF structures makes it difficult to decide which structures to synthesize. Quantum mechanical studies can be used to guide experimental synthesis of materials that are likely to be useful in a given application. We show here that such studies can be used to design a new MOF that should be useful in the separation of dinitrogen from methane, a very difficult separation that is vital for the utilization of natural gas. An open V(II) site in a MOF structure will give a material with a significantly higher enthalpy of adsorption for dinitrogen than for methane, due to stronger selective back bonding to the dinitrogen molecule.
